content.subtitle: Form
bullets: All modals (*will, would, shall, should, can, could, may, might, must*) and the semi-modal *ought to* have only one form.

Modals are followed by the bare infinitive (simple or continuous) or the bare perfect infinitive
eg Toby **should be** very fit by now.
Toby **should have recovered** by now.

The semi-modals *have to* and *need to* change their form depending on person and tense
eg The doctor said I **had/needed to** give up red meat.

rows.example: I **can** run a kilometre in four minutes.

rows.use: Expressing decisions made now about future ability
rows.example: We **can** meet at the gym tomorrow, if you like.
I **could** do fifty press-ups with one hand when I was younger.

rows.use: Expressing ability in present, future or general hypothetical situations
Expressing ability in past hypothetical situations
rows.example: I **could have roasted** the potatoes, but I decided that boiling them was healthier.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: We use *be able to* for the infinitive and other tenses.
✓ I'd love to **be able to** fit into these jeans again! (infinitive)
✓ I'll **be able to** leave hospital in a few weeks, apparently. (future)
✓ I've **been able to** swim since I was five. (present perfect)

rows.use: Asking for and giving permission now, for the future or generally
rows.example: **May / Could / Can** I see the doctor, please?

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: *May* is more polite than *could*, and *could* is more polite than *can*.
We don't usually use a modal to talk about past permission.
✓ I **was allowed to** wear a knee support during the match.
✗ I could wear a knee support during the match.
However, we do use *could* to talk about past permission in reported speech.
✓ The coach said I **could** wear a knee support during the match.

rows.use: Asking for and giving advice now, for the future or generally
rows.example: You **ought to / should** cut down on the amount of red meat you eat.
He **ought to / should have made** more of an effort with his diet.
I **must / have to / need to** pick up that prescription from the chemist on the way home.

rows.use: Expressing lack of obligation or necessity
rows.modal: needn't / don't have to / don't need to
rows.example: You **needn't / don't have to / don't need to** pick up that prescription from the chemist as I'll get it while I'm in town.
I **had to** take the pills three times a day for two weeks.

rows.modal: needn't (+ perfect infinitive) / didn't have to / didn't need to
rows.example: I **needn't have gone / didn't have to go / didn't need to go** to the doctor.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: There is usually no difference in meaning between *must* and *have to*. However, we are sometimes more likely to use *must* for personal obligation and *have to* for external obligation.
We can also use *will have/need to* to express future obligation.
✓ You'll **have/need to** be more careful about what you eat in future.
It is unusual to use *must* for questions. We usually use *have/need to*.
✓ **Do I have/need to** take this medicine before every meal?
*Must* cannot be used as an infinitive. Use *to have to*.
✓ I'd hate **to have to** have injections every day.
✗ I'd hate to must have injections every day.
*Mustn't* and *don't/doesn't have/need to* have different meanings.
✓ You **mustn't** do that! (Don't do that!)
✓ You **don't have/need to** do that. (You can do that if you want to but it's not necessary.)
*Needn't* (+ perfect infinitive) always refers to an action that happened. *Didn't have to* and *didn't need to* can refer to actions that did or didn't happen.
✓ I **needn't have gone** to the doctor. (I went but it wasn't necessary.)
✓ I **didn't have/need to** go to the doctor because I suddenly felt better. (I didn't go.)
✓ I **didn't have/need to** go to the doctor but I went just to be on the safe side. (I did go.)
Be careful with the verb *need*. It can also take the *-ing* form.
✓ I need to sterilise this syringe.
✓ This syringe needs sterilising.

rows.use: Expressing certainty (or near certainty) about now or generally
rows.example: That **must** be the district nurse at the door. These **can't / couldn't** be the pills; they're the wrong colour.

rows.use: Expressing certainty (or near certainty) about the past
rows.example: She **must have been** in a lot of pain. His leg **can't / couldn't have been** in plaster for two years!

rows.use: Expressing probability about now, the future or generally
rows.example: You **ought to / should** feel better in a few days, as long as you get lots of rest.
The bruise **ought to / should have disappeared** days ago. I wonder why it didn't.

rows.use: Expressing possibility about now, the future or generally
rows.example: You should talk to your doctor first because that diet **could / may / might** be dangerous.

rows.use: Expressing possibility about the real past
rows.example: That **could / may / might have been** the doctor who rang earlier while we were out.

rows.use: Expressing possibility about a hypothetical past
rows.example: It's a good thing you went to the doctor or you **could / might have become** quite ill.

sections.title: A: Ability
sections.instructions: Correct the word or phrase in bold. If it is correct, mark OK.

items.sentence: Can you **to speak** French?
I **can** give you a hand tomorrow morning, if you like.
I'll **can** take my driving test after a few more lessons.
Jack **can** play the guitar before he learnt to talk!
If only I **can** afford to buy that top!
We **can** have gone up the Eiffel Tower while we were in Paris, but we decided to go to the Louvre instead.
I could **get** a more expensive computer, but it didn't seem worth it.
I wish I **could** get out of the maths test tomorrow!
I bet you'd love to be **can** to get satellite TV.
You'd better tell the coach if you can't **playing** on Saturday.

sections.title: B: Permission, advice, criticism
sections.instructions: Circle the correct word or phrase. If both options are correct, circle both.

items.sentence: Hello. **Could / Can** I speak to Mrs Johnson, please?
We **could / were allowed to** go home early yesterday because our teacher was ill.
The head teacher said we **could / were allowed to** go home.
Do you think I **should / could be** worried about these spots on my forehead?
You **ought to / should** enter that talent contest!
You **couldn't / shouldn't** talk to people like that! It's rude!
Alan should **write / have written** two essays in the exam yesterday, not one!
No, you **may / should not** go out tonight. You know you're grounded!
Diana should have **waited / been waiting** for me at the corner. I wonder where she went.
What were you doing in the park? You ought to have **done / been doing** your homework then!
Yes, of course you **can / are able** to open the window if you're too hot!

sections.title: C: Obligation and necessity
sections.instructions: Write the correct form of must, have to, need to or needn't in each gap. (More than one answer may be possible.)

items.sentence: Oh, I ...... remember to get some potatoes on the way home tonight.
Jason ...... see the headmaster during the next break. I wonder what it's about?
We ...... light lots of candles during the power cut two nights ago.
I'll start doing my Christmas cards soon. It's nearly December. I ...... start doing my Christmas cards soon.
Carl, you ...... run into the street like that without looking first. It's dangerous!
People with solar-powered cars ...... worry about the price of petrol.
I wouldn't like to ...... get up at five o'clock every morning.
We ...... do any washing-up after the picnic because we'd used disposable plates and cutlery.
Do professional musicians ...... practise every day?
I ...... have bothered cooking all that food; they'd eaten before they arrived.

sections.title: D: Degrees of certainty
sections.instructions: Complete each sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
His lights are on so I'm pretty sure Dan is at home. **as**
Dan ...... his lights are on.

Susie's car's not here so she's almost certainly taking Dobber to the vet. **be**
Susie ...... Dobber to the vet since her car's not here.

There's no way that boy's Simon. He's much taller! **boy**
That ...... Simon. He's much taller!

I'm certain the Winners don't think we're coming tonight; we arranged it for next Tuesday. **expecting**
The Winners ...... tonight; we arranged it for next Tuesday.

I bet you were exhausted after such a long journey! **have**
You ...... exhausted after such a long journey!

The only explanation is that Evan was on the phone to someone in Australia! **talking**
Evan ...... on the phone to someone in Australia!

There's no way Casey won the disco dancing competition - he's got two left feet! **have**
Casey ...... the disco dancing competition - he's got two left feet!

I don't believe Helen's been trying to call us all day. The phone hasn't rung once. **been**
Helen ...... to call us all day. The phone hasn't rung once.

sections.title: E: Matching (Modal meanings)
sections.instructions: Use the words in the box only once to complete the sentences.

items.sentence: I ...... have left my bag on the bus. (expressing certainty)
In a few months, I'll be ...... to buy a car. (expressing future ability)
I ...... drive when I was thirteen years old! (expressing past ability)
No, you ...... have any more pocket money! (refusing a request)
I think you ...... consider a career in the armed forces. (giving advice)
I ...... forget to phone Julie tonight! (expressing personal obligation)
I ...... to have a filling at the dentist's. (expressing external obligation in the past)
You don't ...... to do Exercise D for homework. (expressing a lack of obligation)
You ...... have to work a lot harder if you want to get a good report. (expressing future obligation)
They ...... to arrive at about 8. (expressing probability)
Sean ...... have got stuck in traffic. (expressing possibility)
I ...... have worried so much about Jan's present. She loved it! (expressing a lack of past obligation)
I ...... have a look at those shoes in the window, please?
We ...... pay for the tickets as Josie won them in a competition.
You really ...... make such a mountain out of a molehill!
You won't ...... to connect to the Internet once you've got broadband as you're online twenty-four hours a day.
I hope we ...... find the cinema easily.
We couldn't find a hotel room so we ...... sleep in the car. It was awful!
We'd love to ...... afford to go on a round-the-world cruise.
Fiona can't ...... about the meeting. I reminded her this morning!
She could ...... in the garage when we came round, which would explain why she didn't hear the bell.
You'll ...... tell the police that your house was broken into.

sections.title: G: Modal transformation
sections.instructions: Write a modal or semi-modal in each gap to replace the phrase in brackets. Add any other words you need.

items.sentence: Bruce ...... (is able to) finish most crosswords in under ten minutes.
Charlotte didn't get to the Craig David concert because she ...... (wasn't able to) get tickets.
I ...... (had the opportunity to go) to Oxford but I decided to go to a more modern university.
You ...... (were wrong to tell) Angus. You know he can't keep a secret!
If you have a cashpoint card, you ...... (are not forced to) go into the bank to get money from your account.
We ...... (were made to) apologise to the police for wasting their time.
That ...... (almost definitely wasn't) the last can of soda in the fridge. I bought loads this morning!
We ...... (Were you obliged to) talk about two photographs during the interview?
Children ...... (are not allowed to) be left unattended.
The weather ...... (will probably) be good tomorrow.
It ...... have been a very traumatic experience for her.
I simply ...... have the television on when I'm trying to study! It's too distracting!
We ...... pay for the tickets as we had complimentary passes.
It's ...... be him, he said he'd be here by seven.
You ...... get a new job. You're always complaining about the one you've got.
You ...... pick up the dry cleaning or I won't have a clean shirt for the party.
They ...... have reached the airport yet, it's far too early.
I'm sorry I'm late. I ...... get to the bank before it closed.
You ...... forgot to tell me about the party. I wouldn't have missed it for the world!
It's no problem, you ...... give me the money back until next week.
They say that raising taxes on alcohol ...... reduce the number of alcohol-related diseases.
We ...... to have bought such a big car. We never use the back seats.

sections.title: I: Cloze (Modals)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: When I broke my arm a week ago, I guess it (1) ...... to have hurt. But it didn't!
We (2) ...... to stand on each other's shoulders. I was right at the top.
We (3) ...... have been doing it properly because suddenly the pyramid collapsed and we all fell.
Mr Jenkins (4) ...... have known immediately that I'd broken my arm because he sent someone to call an ambulance.
'Sit still, and don't move your arm at all until the ambulance comes,' he said. I (5) ...... still remember the feeling - my arm was numb, and looked very strange, but there was no pain at all.
I remember thinking: 'It (6) ...... be broken. If it was broken, I'd be in agony.'
I suppose not feeling any pain (7) ...... have been because I was in shock.
The plaster (8) ...... come off in about three weeks but it (9) ...... have to stay on longer.

The next three weeks may (10) ...... be the best three weeks of my life - no basketball, no playing in the playground, no swimming - but I'm counting my blessings. It (11) ...... have been a lot worse!
